bank: Headline with emotionally charged words gets more clicks than headline without.
Headline using numbers or lists receives more clicks than headline without numbers.
Headline framed as a question attracts more clicks than a declarative headline.
Headline with first-person or direct address ('you', 'we') gains more clicks than impersonal headlines.
Headline containing a sense of urgency or exclusivity gets more clicks than neutral headlines.
Shorter headlines get more clicks than longer, wordier headlines.
Headlines that imply secrets or insider information attract more clicks than straightforward headlines.
Headlines with positive sentiment receive more clicks than those with negative sentiment.
Headline using active voice gets more clicks than headline using passive voice.
Headline that teases a surprising or counterintuitive fact gets more clicks than purely factual headlines.
Use of slang or informal language in headlines results in more clicks than formal language.
Headlines that mention a celebrity or well-known figure get more clicks than those that do not.
Headline phrased with a promise of a benefit or solution achieves more clicks than headline without.
Headline with keywords matching trending topics gains more clicks than headline without trending keywords.
Headline that uses alliteration or rhyme gets more clicks than headline with plain phrasing.
